question: [File: 'src/ratifiers/libraries/HashLib.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate / bitmap loop with all 16 collaterals contributing to badDebt] Can an unprivileged liquidator, under the precondition that a borrower has 16 active collaterals all with price == 0 (oracles return 0), call liquidate(seizedAssets=0, repaidUnits=0) and observe that the bitmap loop computes badDebt = originalDebt - sum(0) = originalDebt (since zeroFloorSub(badDebt, 0) == badDebt for each iteration), and maxDebt = 0, so the position is liquidatable (originalDebt > 0 == maxDebt), and badDebt == originalDebt is fully socialized, and assert that after this call debt == 0 and lossFactor is updated correctly? Proof idea: all-zero-price liquidation test asserting full bad debt realization.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/libraries/HashLib.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: isHealthy / bitmap loop with collateral amounts near type(uint128).max] Can an unprivileged borrower, under the precondition that collateral[I] == type(uint128).max and oracle price == ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE (1e36) and LLTV == WAD (1e18), call isHealthy and observe that `collateral[I].mulDivDown(price, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE)` computes type(uint128).max * 1e36 / 1e36 = type(uint128).max, then `.mulDivDown(lltv, WAD)` computes type(uint128).max * 1e18 / 1e18 = type(uint128).max, and maxDebt accumulates type(uint128).max, and if there are multiple such collaterals, maxDebt overflows uint256, causing isHealthy to return an incorrect result? Proof idea: overflow fuzz test with maximum collateral and price values, assert no uint256 overflow in maxDebt.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/libraries/HashLib.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate / bitmap loop with collateralIndex in bitmap but collateral[collateralIndex] == 0] Can an unprivileged liquidator, under the precondition that bit I is set but collateral[I] == 0 (ghost bit), call liquidate(collateralIndex=I, seizedAssets=S>0) and observe that the loop sets liquidatedCollatPrice = oracle[I].price() (nonzero), then after the loop seizedAssets branch computes repaidUnits = S.mulDivUp(liquidatedCollatPrice, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivUp(WAD, lif) > 0, then `newCollateral = position.collateral[I] - S = 0 - S` which underflows and reverts, preventing the liquidation? Proof idea: ghost bit with zero collateral, nonzero seizedAssets, assert arithmetic underflow revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/libraries/HashLib.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: liquidate / bitmap loop with collateralIndex in bitmap and collateral[collateralIndex] == 0 and seizedAssets == 0] Can an unprivileged liquidator, under the precondition that bit I is set but collateral[I] == 0 (ghost bit) and the position is liquidatable, call liquidate(collateralIndex=I, seizedAssets=0, repaidUnits=0) and observe that the loop includes index I in maxDebt computation (contributing 0 since collateral[I]==0) and badDebt computation (zeroFloorSub(badDebt, 0) == badDebt), and the `if (repaidUnits > 0 || seizedAssets > 0)` block is skipped, so the ghost bit is NOT cleared, leaving the bitmap corrupted after the call? Proof idea: ghost bit bad debt realization test, assert bitmap is cleaned up or ghost bit does not affect correctness.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/libraries/HashLib.sol -> Scope: High Collateral bitmap corruption causing health checks or liquidation loops to skip active collateral or include inactive collateral'] [Function: isHealthy + liquidate / cross-function bitmap consistency] Can an unprivileged attacker, by executing any sequence of supplyCollateral, withdrawCollateral, liquidate, repay, take, and multicall operations, reach a state where the collateralBitmap for a borrower has a bit set at index I but position.collateral[I] == 0, or has a bit NOT set at index I but position.collateral[I] > 0, violating the nonZeroCollateralsAreActivated invariant from CollateralBitmap.spec, and if so, what specific sequence of operations achieves this desync? Proof idea: stateful invariant fuzz test over all entry points asserting `forall i in [0,127]: (collateral[i] != 0) == getBit(bitmap, i)` after every operation.
